:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Most felony offenders receive indeterminate sentences that contain a minimum and a maximum term. The maximum term may not be greater than the maximum provided by law, and the minimum term may not be less than the minimum provided by law and may not be more than 90% of the maximum term.10 Offenders with indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole when the minimum term imposed by the court, minus good time earned, is complete. When an inmate begins imprisonment, the Department of Corrections calculates a projected parole eligibility date based on the full potential good time allowance and the minimum sentence. This calculation is not “an award of or entitlement to good time but is merely for administrative purposes.” Inmates who have committed an escape, attempted escape, or assisted others to escape from any institution during a sentence are ineligible for parole on that sentence. Inmates who have committed assault with a deadly weapon on another inmate, an officer, or a staff member during a sentence are also ineligible for parole during that sentence.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 633 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 30% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 77% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 90% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 27% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date
